title: Network isolation between containers on different networks
body:
### Issue Description

Hello Podman team !

I'm deploying several services using podman-compose files. Each service is placed in its own dedicated pod and network. However, I noticed that two containers from different pods and networks can still ping each other and access the exposed services, even though they shouldn't be able to.

Is this normal behavior ?

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. podman network create test1
2. podman network create test2
3. podman run --rm -it --net test1 docker.io/nicolaka/netshoot bash -c "ip -c a;nc -nlvp 12345"

*looks at the ip displayed, in my case 10.89.0.21*

4. podman run --rm -it --net test2 docker.io/nicolaka/netshoot bash -c "nc 10.89.0.21 12345"

The first container receives the incoming connection

### Describe the results you received

I noticed that two containers on separate networks can still communicate with each other, even though they aren't connected to the same network.

### Describe the results you expected

I expected the connection to fail, assuming that Podman would configure iptables rules in the rootless network namespace to prevent inter-container communication across networks.

However, after running `podman unshare --rootless-netns` and inspecting the iptables rules with `iptables -L`, I noticed that the FORWARD chain is accepting all traffic between the two networks `test1 (10.89.0.0/24)` and `test2 (10.89.1.0/24)` in both directions.

```
Chain INPUT (policy ACCEPT)
target     prot opt source               destination         
NETAVARK_INPUT  all  --  anywhere             anywhere             /* netavark firewall rules */

Chain FORWARD (policy ACCEPT)
target     prot opt source               destination         
NETAVARK_FORWARD  all  --  anywhere             anywhere             /* netavark firewall rules */

Chain OUTPUT (policy ACCEPT)
target     prot opt source               destination         

Chain NETAVARK_FORWARD (1 references)
target     prot opt source               destination         
DROP       all  --  anywhere             anywhere             ctstate INVALID
ACCEPT     all  --  anywhere             10.89.0.0/24         ctstate RELATED,ESTABLISHED
ACCEPT     all  --  10.89.0.0/24         anywhere            
ACCEPT     all  --  anywhere             10.89.1.0/24         ctstate RELATED,ESTABLISHED
ACCEPT     all  --  10.89.1.0/24         anywhere            

Chain NETAVARK_INPUT (1 references)
target     prot opt source               destination         
ACCEPT     udp  --  10.89.0.0/24         anywhere             udp dpt:domain
ACCEPT     tcp  --  10.89.0.0/24         anywhere             tcp dpt:domain
ACCEPT     udp  --  10.89.1.0/24         anywhere             udp dpt:domain
ACCEPT     tcp  --  10.89.1.0/24         anywhere             tcp dpt:domain

Chain NETAVARK_ISOLATION_2 (1 references)
target     prot opt source               destination         

Chain NETAVARK_ISOLATION_3 (0 references)
target     prot opt source               destination         
DROP       all  --  anywhere             anywhere            
DROP       all  --  anywhere             anywhere            
NETAVARK_ISOLATION_2  all  --  anywhere             anywhere
```

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

I am running the latest version of podman (5.6.0) on Archlinux.

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body: you have to use `podman network create --opt isolate=strict`, I am considering to change the default to isolate by default for podman 6.0.

Thank you so much Luap for your prompt and thorough response.

I wasn't aware of the `isolate=strict` argument. I can confirm that it does indeed update the iptables rule set.
The isolation chains are now active and correctly blocking traffic between my containers.

Honestly, I had assumed that different networks would be isolated by default, but I can see the reasoning behind having everything open unless security is enforced.

Thanks again for your help, you're officially my hero of the day! 😃

```
Chain FORWARD (policy ACCEPT)
target     prot opt source               destination         
NETAVARK_ISOLATION_1  all  --  anywhere             anywhere            
NETAVARK_FORWARD  all  --  anywhere             anywhere             /* netavark firewall rules */

...

Chain NETAVARK_ISOLATION_1 (1 references)
target     prot opt source               destination         
NETAVARK_ISOLATION_3  all  --  anywhere             anywhere            
NETAVARK_ISOLATION_3  all  --  anywhere             anywhere            

Chain NETAVARK_ISOLATION_2 (1 references)
target     prot opt source               destination         
DROP       all  --  anywhere             anywhere            
DROP       all  --  anywhere             anywhere            

Chain NETAVARK_ISOLATION_3 (2 references)
target     prot opt source               destination         
NETAVARK_ISOLATION_2  all  --  anywhere             anywhere
```
